title: Quantum chemistry may not be the “killer app” for quantum computers after all | New Scientist
why:
- Two leading quantum computing algorithms for quantum chemistry—variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) and quantum phase estimation (QPE)—may have very limited practical use, even as quantum hardware improves.
- For VQE to match the accuracy of classical chemistry algorithms, quantum computers would need to be virtually error-free (fault-tolerant), a technology that does not yet exist.
- Even with future fault-tolerant quantum computers, QPE faces an “orthogonality catastrophe,” where its effectiveness for calculating molecular energy drops exponentially as molecule size increases.
- The study calls into question the widely held belief that quantum chemistry will be a “killer application” for quantum computers, highlighting significant ongoing challenges even in a fault-tolerant era.
- Quantum computers may still have potential in chemistry for tasks other than energy calculations, such as simulating how systems respond to external perturbations.
